chunk_summary: This section addresses counseling strategies for Indian households facing the double burden of malnutrition, emphasizing tailored dietary interventions for undernourished and overweight children while promoting family-level meal adjustments.
learning_objectives: Understand the concept of the double burden of malnutrition in Indian households.
Learn dietary strategies to address undernutrition using minimally processed, nutrient-dense foods.
Identify key ultra-processed food drivers contributing to childhood obesity and strategies to reduce their impact.
Develop unified household-level meal plans to balance nutritional needs for all family members.
key_facts: The double burden includes one child with undernutrition and another with overweight, or the same child with stunting and high BMI.
Undernourished children should consume energy-dense, minimally processed foods like ghee, eggs, milk, dal, and seasonal fruits/vegetables.
Overweight children should reduce sugary drinks, packaged snacks, and large portions of fried/ready-to-eat foods.
Family meals should be unified, with nutrient-dense sides for undernourished children and reduced snack frequency for overweight children.
Growth curves and diet history are essential tools for assessing primary risks in children.
Follow-up should focus on growth, school performance, and family feasibility rather than rapid weight changes.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: "Double-duty actions for nutrition aim to address both undernutrition and obesity within the same population, often within the same household." (Source: WHO policy brief)
document_key_topics: Double burden of malnutrition in Indian households
Reducing ultra-processed food exposure in overweight children
Household-level meal planning for diverse nutritional needs
